page: Vultr vx1-m-8c-64gb is a memory-optimized instance belonging to the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features an x86_64 AMD EPYC processor running at 2.0 GHz, configured with 8 shared vCPUs derived from 4 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. The instance is equipped with 64.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. Storage is network-based with a minimal local capacity of 1 GB, and there are no integrated GPUs.

Benchmark data indicates top-tier performance in single-core operations, with stress-ng single-core CPU performance, single-threaded gzip compression, and single-threaded bzip3 decompression all scoring in the top 10%. However, multi-core performance and memory bandwidth tests, including large-block reads and writes, fall into the average tier. This pattern is also reflected in LLM inference, where text generation with a small 135M model achieves top-tier speeds, while prompt processing with a medium 7B model is weak, placing in the bottom 25%.

Given its shared CPU allocation, the vx1-m-8c-64gb offers a cost-efficient profile for memory-intensive workloads that do not require sustained, dedicated CPU performance. The high resource density of 64.0 GB RAM supports memory-heavy applications, caching layers, and database operations that fit within its memory footprint, though the shared CPU architecture and network-bound storage present tradeoffs for sustained high-throughput computing.
description: Vultr vx1-m-8c-64gb is a memory-optimized server from the VX1 Cloud Compute family, featuring an x86_64 AMD EPYC processor with 8 shared vCPUs and 64.0 GB of RAM. Lacking a GPU and utilizing 1 GB of network-based local storage, this instance delivers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Performance benchmarks show top-tier single-core CPU execution and single-threaded compression speeds, alongside average multi-core and memory bandwidth results. Due to its shared CPU allocation, it provides a cost-effective option for memory-intensive workloads. It fits caching layers, database operations, and memory-heavy applications that do not require dedicated, sustained multi-core processing.
